narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 54.9% chance that you would not pass—crossing the threshold we use to make admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, LSAT score of 32.50, Year 1 grade decile of 4, Year 3 decile of 6, upper-middle family income, and full-time enrollment plans. Among students who passed the bar, the average LSAT is 37.27 and undergraduate GPA is 3.24, while your Year 1 decile sits notably below their average of 6.04. The three key factors driving this prediction were your Year 3 grade decile, LSAT score, and Year 1 grade decile, ranked by their influence on the model's decision. Most encouragingly, your stronger Year 3 performance pushed the prediction toward expecting you to pass, showing real improvement that the model recognized as a positive signal. However, your LSAT score had the opposite effect, pulling strongly toward a failure prediction since it falls short of what's typical for bar passers. Similarly, your lower Year 1 decile contributed toward the failure outlook, highlighting early challenges that weighed against you despite later gains. Compared to admitted students, who often enter full-time with higher early academic deciles and LSATs aligning closer to 37, your profile appeared riskier in these core areas, even with your solid family background fitting the upper-middle range common among passers. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's the model weighing patterns from past students to protect the program's bar passage rates. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the 37+ range seen in successful applicants, and reflect on strategies to elevate your early-grade performance if reapplying. Strengthening these could shift future predictions in your favor, opening doors to programs that value your upward trajectory. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by targeting these exact areas, and with your full-time commitment, you're well-positioned to do the same.
